start.text: Mr. Crane, the caretaker, polishes a spotless patch of the dusty front desk. 'Welcome to the Grand Palm Resort... or what's left of it. You kids shouldn't be here after dark. The Phantom doesn't like guests.'

choices.text: 'The ledger says supplies were billed to Room 217 — which doesn't exist.'
'Explain the deed in your safe. The one selling YOUR resort to YOU.'

ghost.text: 'Glows green as swamp gas, wails like a busted pipe organ. It's scared off every buyer I've shown the property to. Some nights I see it drifting across the grounds toward the old boiler room...'

janitor.text: 'Gus? Harmless enough. Keeps to himself. Though he does spend an awful lot of time in that locked closet of his. Lost his key recently, or so he claims.'

sale.text: Crane's eye twitches. 'Between us? If the resort doesn't sell soon, the bank takes everything. Though with the hauntings, the price keeps dropping... and dropping...' He smiles faintly, then catches himself.

ledger_press.text: Crane goes very still. 'You read the ledger? Nosy little... ahem. A clerical error, nothing more. Room 217 was — water damaged. Yes. Don't go poking around the boiler room, it isn't SAFE.' Funny — nobody had said a word about any boiler room.

deed_press.text: Crane's polishing rag stops mid-circle. For three full seconds the lobby is very, very quiet. 'You opened my safe.' He smiles like a crack spreading across ice. 'Enjoy the resort while you can, children. Its NEW OWNER may not be so... hospitable.'

start.text: Janitor Gus stops raking and eyes you warily over the dead leaves. 'Resort's closed, kids. Nothin' out here but weeds and bad memories.'

choices.text: Show him the can of glowing paint

ghost.text: 'Ain't no ghost.' He grips the rake tighter. 'Just drafts, bad wiring, and folks seein' what they're told to see. Now scram before you get hurt. Or... before somethin' hurts you.'

shed.text: 'Toolshed? Dropped my maintenance key in there weeks back. Too dark to find it, and the boss won't spring for a new bulb. If you fish it out, don't go usin' it on my closet, y'hear?'

confront.text: You hold up the can of LUMO-GLOW paint. Gus goes white as a — well, you know. 'Where'd you— okay, OKAY! I bought that paint, but only 'cause MR. CRANE told me to! Said it was for renovatin' the pool mural.' He leans on his rake. 'Six cans of ghost-green paint... for ONE mural. I just rake here, kids. Do your own arithmetic — I ain't takin' the fall for nobody!'

choices.text: 'Six cans. One mural. That math is going straight in the case notes, Gus.'

truth.text: Gus nods slowly. 'That's what I know, and I'll say it to the police word for word. You want the WHY of it, that's paperwork — and paperwork lives in the manager's office, top of the lobby stairs. Take my spare passkey. And kids... lately I hear BANGING behind that office wall, and my closet floor grate rattlin' after midnight. Buildings ain't supposed to do either.'

choices.text: 'Thanks, Gus. Gang — we've got an office to search!'

spare_key.text: 'Figured you'd ask.' Gus fishes a brass passkey from his overalls. 'Spare passkey — opens the office at the top of the lobby stairs. And listen... some nights I hear BANGING behind his office wall. Walls ain't supposed to bang.'

start.text: You yank the lever! The Phantom Guest — mid-lunge at the Scooby Snack — is snatched into the laundry net and hauled kicking toward the ceiling! 'RELEASE ME, MEDDLERS! THE PHANTOM CURSES YOU ALLLL!' Fred wipes away a proud tear: 'Gang... that snare was BEAUTIFUL.'

whodunit.text: The phantom writhes in the net, sheet still on. Velma adjusts her glasses: 'Hold on, gang — a proper unmasking needs a proper ACCUSATION. Think about the evidence... WHO is under that sheet?'

choices.text: 'Like, it's obviously Janitor Gus! It's HIS closet!'
'It's George Palm! He wants the resort for himself!'
'It's Mr. Crane. The ledger, the paint invoice, the boiler room — it all fits.'

wrong_gus.text: Daphne glances out the boiler-room grate... Gus is STILL up on the grounds, raking leaves in the moonlight. He waves back, sarcastically. Velma sighs: 'Also, the phantom is a full head taller than Gus. The boots were size 12 — Gus wears galoshes. Try again.'

wrong_ghost.text: Shaggy and Scooby are already halfway up a steam pipe! Inside the net, the 'ghost' rolls its eyes and checks its wristwatch. Velma taps her foot: 'Ghosts do not wear GRAND PALM STAFF watches, you two. Get down here.'

reveal.text: Velma yanks off the glowing sheet... 'MR. CRANE?!' The caretaker sags in the net as George Palm III steps forward with the deed draft. 'Scare off every buyer, bankrupt the estate, then buy paradise for pennies — with MY family's money troubles as cover. And the Wail-o-Phone, Ambrose? Really?' Crane thrashes: 'And I would have gotten away with it too, if it weren't for you meddling kids, the mangy mutt, and the HEIR I HAD TIED TO A CHAIR!' Scooby: 'ROOBY-ROOBY-ROO!'

wrong_george.text: A voice from the stairwell: 'I can hear you, you know.' George Palm III walks in, STILL picking clothesline out of his hair. Velma: 'The phantom tied George up because George refused to sell. Victims rarely haunt themselves. Think about who profits when the price hits rock bottom...'

start.text: You slice the clothesline and the young man bursts free, spitting out a sock. 'FINALLY! George Palm — the third. I'm the one who WROTE you, gang — I hired Mystery Inc.! I came back to reopen my grandfather's resort, not sell it. Two nights ago I followed the "phantom" up here... and the phantom locked me in my own grandfather's secret suite. Some welcome for your client, huh?'

choices.text: 'Did you see who it was under the sheet?'
'Wait — reopen it? Crane says it has to sell.'

motive.text: 'Sell it? To WHO?' George's jaw tightens. 'The only offer on the table is some lowball buyer nobody's ever met. As the heir I was about to refuse it and reopen the Grand Palm myself. Funny thing — the hauntings started the same week I announced that.'

choices.text: 'Did you see who it was under the sheet?'

witness.text: 'Better. When he shoved me in here, his sheet snagged the door and lifted.' George looks grim. 'Silver hair. Staff watch. And a keyring that jingled like sleigh bells — the master ring only ONE person at this resort carries. I'll testify to the police, but you'll need to catch him in the act.'

choices.text: 'Then we build a trap. Stay hidden — and we'll take that clothesline he tied you with. Fred collects rope.'
'We're on it. Stay hidden until you hear the net spring!'

start.text: A small woman in a pressed uniform folds the same striped towel, again. 'Oh! GUESTS! We haven't had guests since... well.' She smooths her apron. 'Rosa Delgado, housekeeping. Forty years. And no, I am not leaving because of some glow-in-the-dark bedsheet.'

choices.text: Ask about the resort's glory days
'About this dry-cleaned blazer in the staff locker...'
Let her get back to her towel.

heyday.text: 'You should have SEEN it in '69, niños. Orchestras by the pool. Movie stars in the cabana. Mr. Palm Senior kept Room 217 as his own private suite — best view on the coast. He said the Grand Palm would outlive us all.' She sighs at the towel. 'It is certainly outliving its reputation.'

wail.text: 'I hear it some nights — that awful pipe-organ wail. But here is the thing.' She leans in. 'It always comes from UNDER my cabana. Under the deck, under the pool. Ghosts do not take the service tunnels, niños. STAFF take the service tunnels.'

crane.text: 'Señor Crane still has me press his staff blazer every single week. Very particular. Creases like knife edges.' She folds the towel with sudden violence. 'Where a caretaker goes at MIDNIGHT that needs a pressed blazer, I could not tell you.'

ticket.text: 'Sí, that is my ticket — I sign them A.C., for Ambrose Crane. Every week, like the resort never closed.' Daphne beams: 'The laundry never lies. NEVER.'

start.text: A wiry old man in coveralls jumps, then relaxes, oil can still raised like a duelling pistol. 'Madre— you scared me! Marco. Forty years keeping this place running. Officially retired.' He pats the workbench. 'Unofficially... somebody has to keep her humming.'

choices.text: 'We, uh, borrowed a magnet from your toolbox.'

tools.text: 'Funny thing. My bolt cutters and my paint sprayer walked off about a month back — right when the haunting got AMBITIOUS. And somebody keeps borrowing my padlock oil.' He squints. 'Thieves don't usually MAINTAIN what they steal from, eh?'

pumps.text: 'Those pumps could run TODAY — I keep them oiled, don't tell Crane. HE ordered them shut down, you know. First rule of resorts: a dry pool is a dead resort.' He wipes his hands slowly. 'It is like the man WANTS her ugly.'

boiler.text: 'Some nights the boiler kicks on at two in the morning. I never light her. So either this place has a very COZY ghost...' he taps his temple, '...or somebody is living down there. Warm, private, and paid for.'

toolbox.text: 'My little red one! Keep the magnet — it isn't mine. Somebody's been using MY shed as THEIR locker.' He grins suddenly. 'And kid — every socket on this property is the same size. I standardized them in '81. One crank fits ALL of it. You're welcome.'

start.text: An old man in a straw hat waters a fern that is frankly beyond help. 'Shhh. They are listening.' He tips the hat. 'Tomás. I keep the green things. KEPT,' he corrects himself, sadly, 'the green things.'

choices.text: Ask if he's seen the Phantom
'We found a green thread on your roses...'

flowers.text: 'Palm Senior planted half these beds with his own hands. The moonflowers were his favorite — they only open for the night.' He frowns. 'Lately they open at NINE SHARP. Every night. Flowers do not lie about visitors, niños. Something walks my greenhouse on a schedule.'

walker.text: 'Seen him? Señorita, I watched your terrible phantom catch his sheet on my roses, TRIP over my good urn, and say a word no ghost should know.' He sips from a thermos. 'Ghosts float. Frauds stumble.'

topiary.text: 'The scary bush? I trimmed it like that as a JOKE in 1975. Palm Senior laughed so hard he gave me Fridays off, forever.' He looks at it fondly. 'Nobody remembers the joke now. Only the fear. That is resorts for you.'

thread.text: 'From my roses, sí — shoulder height on the thorns.' He holds a flat hand up to demonstrate, then raises it a notch. 'Your ghost is exactly one CRANE tall. I have trimmed hedges shorter than that man's alibi.'

start.text: The temperature drops ten degrees. By Pump No. 1 stands a silver, translucent gentleman in a 1969 suit and straw boater. Shaggy and Scooby stack themselves into one trembling totem pole. 'Peace, gentlemen. George Palm, SENIOR. Yes — a real ghost. No, I do not wail.' He straightens his bow tie. 'That RACKET upstairs is an impostor, and frankly, it embarrasses the whole profession.'

choices.text: 'We've got a case file full of evidence — and a trap ready for tonight.'
Back away. Politely. (Shaggy is already gone.)

impostor.text: 'A man in a painted BEDSHEET, using my resort's good name to frighten away buyers. He walks MY service corridors like he owns them.' The pumps rattle softly. 'He does not. Not yet — and if you children are half as clever as you look, not ever.'

tunnels.text: 'I built these corridors in 1969 — pumps to laundry to boiler. A good host is never SEEN pushing a maintenance cart.' He allows himself a small smile. 'I was so proud, I printed the full corridor plan in my grand-opening BROCHURE. Vanity. But perhaps useful vanity, hmm?'

family.text: 'My grandson came home to REOPEN my paradise. Then he vanished into the walls — some nights I hear him BANGING, poor boy, somewhere above the office.' The glow flickers. 'Find him. Palms keep their promises. It's the family curse — the good kind.'

blessing.text: 'Then spring it WELL.' He rises an inch off the floor, which is somehow formal. 'And when the sheet comes off, tell the man under it this, word for word: the Palms never sold.' He tips the boater. 'He will know exactly what it means.' The pump-light flickers, and he is gone.

choices.text: '...Like, did anybody else just get chills?' (Leave)
